title: Pulicaria viscosa
intro: Pulicaria viscosa, also known as sticky fleabane, is a perennial herb native to the Mediterranean region, characterized by its sticky leaves and yellowish flowers. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health issues, including fever, cough, and skin conditions, due to its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antiseptic properties. The bioactive constituents of Pulicaria viscosa include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Pulicaria viscosa include infusions, decoctions, and tinctures, which are often used to treat respiratory issues, wounds, and skin infections.
taxonomy: Pulicaria viscosa is classified within the family Asteraceae, commonly known as the daisy or sunflower family. This classification places it within the order Asterales. The family Asteraceae is a large and diverse group, with over 23,000 species. Within the family Asteraceae, Pulicaria viscosa is further classified within the tribe Inuleae. This tribe is a subfamily of the daisy family that contains many species of inuleae plants.
uses: Pulicaria viscosa is traditionally used in various cultures for its medicinal properties. The plant's leaves and stems are said to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects, making it useful for treating wounds, cuts, and skin irritations. In some parts of the world, Pulicaria viscosa is used to treat digestive issues, such as diarrhea and nausea. The plant's extracts are also believed to have antioxidant properties, which may help protect against cell damage and oxidative stress. Further research is needed to fully understand the potential health benefits of Pulicaria viscosa.

The plant also contains sesquiterpene lactones, such as pulchellin B and pulicarin, which have been reported to exhibit anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities.
